result.markdown:
--- title: "Examples" subtitle: "Every function, demonstrated" --- ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(graphfast) library(data.table) set.seed(1) ``` ::: This page walks through every exported function with small, self-contained examples. The chunks are live â€” the output you see is produced when the site is rendered. A graph in `graphfast` is just a **two-column edge list**: a matrix or `data.table` where each row is an edge `from -> to`. We'll reuse this little graph with three components throughout: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} edges <- matrix(c( 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, # component A: {1,2,3,4} 5, 6, # component B: {5,6} 7, 8, 8, 9 # component C: {7,8,9} ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) edges ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [,1] [,2] [1,] 1 2 [2,] 2 3 [3,] 3 4 [4,] 5 6 [5,] 7 8 [6,] 8 9 ``` ::: ::: # Connected components ## `find_connected_components()` The workhorse. Returns one component id **per node**, the size of each component, and the total count. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cc <- find_connected_components(edges) cc$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cc$component_sizes ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 4 2 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cc$components # component id for node 1, 2, 3, ... ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 1 1 2 2 3 3 3 ``` ::: ::: Node 5 and node 6 share component 2; node 1 is in component 1: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cc$components[c(1, 5, 6)] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 2 2 ``` ::: ::: `compress = FALSE` keeps the raw Unionâ€“Find root labels instead of tidy consecutive ids (the counts are still correct): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} raw <- find_connected_components(edges, compress = FALSE) raw$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} head(raw$components) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0 0 0 0 4 4 ``` ::: ::: You can pass `n_nodes` explicitly to skip the scan for the maximum id (handy when you already know it, or to include isolated nodes with no edges): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} find_connected_components(edges, n_nodes = 12)$n_components # nodes 10,11,12 are singletons ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 6 ``` ::: ::: ## `find_connected_components_safe()` When node ids are **sparse or large**, a dense `parent` array up to the maximum id would waste memory. This version remaps ids to `1..(unique nodes)` with `fastmatch`, runs the algorithm, then maps the answer back. The returned `components` vector is **named by the original ids**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sparse_edges <- matrix(c( 1000, 2000, 2000, 3000, 9000, 9001 ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) res <- find_connected_components_safe(sparse_edges, verbose = FALSE) res$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res$components # names are the original node ids ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 1000 2000 3000 9000 9001 1 1 1 2 2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res$memory_info$memory_saved_gb ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.0001005381 ``` ::: ::: ## `find_connected_components_large()` Handles ids **beyond the 32-bit integer limit** (â‰ˆ 2.1e9) by keeping them as doubles until after remapping. Set `verbose = TRUE` to see what it decided. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} big_edges <- matrix(c( 1e10, 2e10, 2e10, 3e10, 7e10, 8e10 ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) big <- find_connected_components_large(big_edges, verbose = FALSE) big$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} big$components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 1e+10 2e+10 3e+10 7e+10 8e+10 1 1 1 2 2 ``` ::: ::: # Per-edge components Often you don't want a node-indexed vector â€” you want a component id attached to each **edge / row** of your data. ## `get_edge_components()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ec <- get_edge_components(edges) ec$from_components # component of each edge's 'from' node ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 1 2 3 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ec$to_components # ... and its 'to' node (always equal for a real edge) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 1 2 3 3 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ec$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 ``` ::: ::: `return_type = "combined"` returns just the `from_components` vector (same length as the number of edges): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} get_edge_components(edges, return_type = "combined") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 1 2 3 3 ``` ::: ::: ## `group_edges()` and `edge_components()` `group_edges()` is the convenience alias returning one component id per edge row. `edge_components()` does the same but takes a `data.table` and column names, so it drops straight into a `data.table` expression. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Matrix in -> vector out group_edges(edges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 1 2 3 3 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt <- data.table(from = c(1, 2, 5), to = c(2, 3, 6)) dt[, component := edge_components(.SD, "from", "to")] dt ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| from| to| component| |----:|--:|---------:| | 1| 2| 1| | 2| 3| 1| | 5| 6| 3|
::: ::: ## `add_component_column()` Adds a component column to a `data.table` **by reference** (no copy), mapping node ids with `fastmatch` internally. Works with any column names. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt2 <- data.table(source = c(1, 2, 5, 7), target = c(2, 3, 6, 8), weight = runif(4)) add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| source| target| weight| component| |------:|------:|---------:|---------:| | 1| 2| 0.2655087| 1| | 2| 3| 0.3721239| 1| | 5| 6| 0.5728534| 2| | 7| 8| 0.9082078| 3|
::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt2 ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| source| target| weight| component| |------:|------:|---------:|---------:| | 1| 2| 0.2655087| 1| | 2| 3| 0.3721239| 1| | 5| 6| 0.5728534| 2| | 7| 8| 0.9082078| 3|
::: ::: Use `in_place = FALSE` to leave the original untouched and get a copy back, and `verbose = TRUE` for per-step timing on large data: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} out <- add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target", component_col = "grp", in_place = FALSE) out ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| source| target| weight| component| grp| |------:|------:|---------:|---------:|---:| | 1| 2| 0.2655087| 1| 1| | 2| 3| 0.3721239| 1| 1| | 5| 6| 0.5728534| 2| 2| | 7| 8| 0.9082078| 3| 3|
::: ::: # Connectivity and distance ## `are_connected()` Batched "are these two nodes in the same component?" queries. Builds Unionâ€“Find once, then answers each pair in near-constant time. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} queries <- matrix(c( 1, 4, # same component A 1, 5, # A vs B 7, 9 # same component C ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) are_connected(edges, queries) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE FALSE TRUE ``` ::: ::: ## `shortest_paths()` Hop-distance (unweighted BFS) for each query pair. `-1` means no path exists. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} shortest_paths(edges, queries) # 1->4 is 3 hops; 1->5 unreachable; 7->9 is 2 hops ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 3 -1 2 ``` ::: ::: `max_distance` stops the search early â€” anything farther returns `-1`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} shortest_paths(edges, matrix(c(1, 4), ncol = 2), max_distance = 2) # 1->4 needs 3 hops ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] -1 ``` ::: ::: ## `graph_statistics()` Degree summary and density in a single pass â€” no adjacency matrix. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} stats <- graph_statistics(edges) stats$n_nodes ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 9 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$n_edges ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 6 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$density ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 0.1666667 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$degree_stats ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $min [1] 1 $max [1] 2 $mean [1] 1.333333 ``` ::: ::: # Entity resolution / grouping These solve "group rows that share a value across any of these columns" as a connected-components problem. ## `group_id()` The general engine. Give it a `data.frame`/`data.table` and the columns to match on (regex by default), plus values that should never glue rows together (`incomparables`). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} customers <- data.frame( id = 1:6, phone1 = c("123-456-7890", "987-654-3210", "123-456-7890", "", "555-0123", ""), phone2 = c("", "987-654-3210", "555-1234", "123-456-7890","", "555-0123"), email = c("a@x.com", "b@x.com", "c@x.com", "a@x.com", "d@x.com", "d@x.com"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) # cols = "phone" matches phone1 + phone2 by regex; "" is incomparable group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c("")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 2 1 1 3 3 ``` ::: ::: Match across all three columns by passing exact names (`use_regex = FALSE`): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c("")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 2 1 1 3 3 ``` ::: ::: `return_details = TRUE` returns a rich object with a `print` method showing which shared values created which groups: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} res <- group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c(""), return_details = TRUE) res ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multi-Column Group ID Results ============================= Total rows: 6 Number of groups: 3 Group sizes: 3, 1, 2 Columns used: 3 Case sensitive: TRUE Min group size: 1 Incomparables: Group IDs (first 20): [1] 1 2 1 1 3 3 Shared values creating groups (first 10): 'd@x.com': rows 5, 6 '123-456-7890': rows 1, 3, 4 '987-654-3210': rows 2, 2 '555-0123': rows 5, 6 'a@x.com': rows 1, 4 ``` ::: ::: Other useful arguments: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Only keep groups of at least 2 (singletons get id 0) group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c(""), min_group_size = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 0 1 1 2 2 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Case-insensitive matching group_id(data.frame(a = c("ABC", "abc", "xyz")), case_sensitive = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1 1 2 ``` ::: ::: ## `add_group_ids()` The `data.table` wrapper for `group_id()` â€” adds the group column by reference. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_cust <- as.data.table(customers) add_group_ids(dt_cust, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), group_col = "entity_id", use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c("")) dt_cust ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 |email | entity_id| |--:|:------------|:------------|:-------|---------:| | 1|123-456-7890 | |a@x.com | 1| | 2|987-654-3210 |987-654-3210 |b@x.com | 2| | 3|123-456-7890 |555-1234 |c@x.com | 1| | 4| |123-456-7890 |a@x.com | 1| | 5|555-0123 | |d@x.com | 3| | 6| |555-0123 |d@x.com | 3|
::: ::: ## `set_group_id()` An alternative grouping engine using the **bipartite edge reduction** (melt â†’ edges â†’ components). It requires an `id` column and writes the group id back by reference. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_sg <- data.table( id = 1:5, phone1 = c("123", "456", "123", "789", "456"), phone2 = c("111", "222", "333", "111", "222") ) set_group_id(dt_sg, cols = "phone", var_output_name = "gid") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 | gid| |--:|:------|:------|---:| | 1|123 |111 | 1| | 2|456 |222 | 2| | 3|123 |333 | 1| | 4|789 |111 | 1| | 5|456 |222 | 2|
::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_sg ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 | gid| |--:|:------|:------|---:| | 1|123 |111 | 1| | 2|456 |222 | 2| | 3|123 |333 | 1| | 4|789 |111 | 1| | 5|456 |222 | 2|
::: ::: # Putting it together A realistic `data.table` flow: take an edge list, attach components, and inspect the largest blob â€” all without ever building a graph object. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} g <- data.table( from = sample(1:50, 200, replace = TRUE), to = sample(1:50, 200, replace = TRUE) ) g <- g[from != to] g[, component := edge_components(.SD, "from", "to")] # Size of each component, largest first sizes <- g[, .N, by = component][order(-N)] head(sizes) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| component| N| |---------:|---:| | 1| 195|
::: ::: See [Benchmarks](benchmarks.qmd) for how these scale to millions and hundreds of millions of edges, and [Reference](reference.qmd) for the full argument lists.
